text: Welcome to this video presentation on Maryland Thoracic Ligature and Signia Power Rectupper Rubectomy performed by Mr. Felice Granato at Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust.

The surgeon utilizes a ligature device for soft tissue dissection and vessel ligation, capable of handling vessels up to seven millimeters in diameter, during a three-port anterior approach for early-stage lung cancer.
The surgeon staples the main branches of the pulmonary artery and pulmonary vein using Medtronic Signia staplers, securing these vessels during the lobectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a ligature to divide the pulmonary ligament, performing both blunt and sharp dissection up to the inferior pulmonary vein. The station line lymph node is harvested, and the presence of the inferior pulmonary vein is confirmed. Adhesions between the pericardium and the lung are released, allowing the lung to be mobilized posteriorly.
The surgeon performs dissection around the superior and middle pulmonary veins, using surgical instruments to carefully separate these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses blunt and sharp dissection techniques to open the mediastinal pleura reflection, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon provides a clear view of the middle pulmonary vein and the superior pulmonary vein, highlighting their anatomical positions and relationships within the surgical field.
The ligature uses bipolar energies, therefore the thermic energy is not dissipated outside the jaws of the instrument, reducing the risk of damaging surrounding tissues.
The surgeon harvests station eleven lymph nodes over V1, then proceeds to encircle and divide the superior pulmonary vein using a Medtronic Signia powered stapler.
which combines the performance of the tripostaper with the feedback on tissues and articulation and firing with one hand.
With the pulmonary vein divided, the anatomy of the hilum is visible, showing the ongoing pulmonary artery and the truncus anterior. The stamped pulmonary vein is visible at the bottom of the screen.
The surgeon harvests station XI lymph nodes to free the apical trunk, which is then dissected using the triplostaper device.
As mentioned, the ligature does not dissipate energy outside the jaws and this minimizes any risk of scalding of the surrounding tissues.
On the other hand, extreme care is needed when close to metal as this can interfere with the metal.

It can be used with the coagulation without actually using the blade.
The surgeon divides the truncus anterior using a signal stapler, completing the separation of this vascular structure.

The ligature has been specifically approved for pulmonary vasculature for vessels up to seven millimeters of diameter but specifically for pulmonary vascular of seventeen millimeters such as a vasculature which is high flow but low pressure with vessel which have a thin wall.
The surgeon identifies bulky lymph nodes located just below the apical trunk, preparing for further dissection or removal.
The surgeon identifies a small bronchial artery behind the current dissection area, which is sealed using a ligature device, conserving one of the reloads of the tripost taper.

The surgeon performs dissection on the posterior mediastinal reflection below the azygous vein, dividing the posterior ascending branch of the right upper lobe A-II to facilitate dissection around the right upper lobe bronchus.
with the adoption of the League Assurer.
The surgeon identifies a vessel less than seven millimeters in diameter, safely dissecting and dividing it using the League Assurer device.
For peace of mind we applied also an hemolog as the surgeon will be able to sleep better at night.
The surgeon identifies the bronchus for the upper lobe, slings it, and divides it following an inflation test.
Although the anatomy was quite clear, it is sometimes useful to perform an inflation test.
The surgeon uses a League Assurer device to carefully dissect and divide a vessel less than seven millimeters in diameter, ensuring the bronchial tissues are not crunched during the process.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the pulmonary fissure using part four reloads, ensuring the separation of the lung lobes.
The surgeon completes the fissure with four reloads, preparing the specimen for delivery into a retrieval bag through the utility port.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy using a ligature for both blunt and sharp dissection on the mediastinum, focusing on harvesting lymph nodes from station IV-R and II-R. The dissection is conducted above the azygos vein to clear the area of its contents.
The surgeon uses a ligature device on small lymphatic vessels to achieve lymphostatic and hemostatic effects, visibly controlling bleeding and lymphatic flow in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy on station II-R and IV-R, carefully dissecting and removing lymphatic tissue from these mediastinal regions.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect lymph nodes at station seven, continuing the lymphadenectomy process within the mediastinum.
The surgeon retracts the lung superiorly and performs dissection above the superior margin of the inferior pulmonary vein, targeting the subcarinal region to harvest station seven lymph nodes.
The surgeon uses the ligature device to achieve hemostasis, controlling bleeding in the surgical field during the lymphadenectomy procedure.
The surgical field is prepared for washing, followed by the placement of a drain. The surgeon is ready to close the incision site.

To conclude, the LigaShield provides a good hemostatic results of small-caliber vessels with a reduction of usage of disposable instruments, a reduction of stepper's reload, offers a safe blunt and sharp dissection of vascular and bronchial structure and allows a quick lymph node dissection.
